paragraphs.body: She left him. The concubine walked out of the Levite's house and went back to her father in Bethlehem. The text says she was angry with him. It does not say what he did. Four months passed. The Levite packed a donkey, took a servant, and traveled to Bethlehem to bring her back. He knocked on the door of a house that was not his.

Her father opened the door and his face changed. He pulled the Levite inside immediately. Fed him. Gave him wine. Asked him to stay another day. Then another. The father kept finding reasons to delay the departure. He pressed food into the Levite's hands every morning. The woman moved around the edges of these meals and nobody recorded what her face looked like.

On the fifth day the Levite stood up before noon and refused to stay longer. He loaded the donkey. The woman climbed on. They left Bethlehem in the afternoon heat with the sun already tilting west, too late to reach home safely. The servant suggested stopping in Jebus. The Levite said no. Jebus was a city of foreigners. He wanted GIBEAH. His own people. Israelites.

They sat in the open square of Gibeah as the sky went dark. No one took them in. The stone of the square was still warm from the day's heat under the Levite's hands. Finally an old man coming in from the fields stopped and looked at them. He asked where they were going. He said: do not spend the night in the square. He brought them into his house. He gave them water to wash the road dust off their feet. He fed them.

Men came from the city and surrounded the house. They beat on the door with their fists. The sound was heavy and rhythmic, the wood shaking in its frame. They demanded the Levite be sent out. The old man went to the door. He told them no. He offered his own virgin daughter instead. He offered the concubine. The men outside would not listen to him.

The Levite took the concubine and put her outside the door. The Bible uses that verb. PUT. The door closed behind her. Inside the house the lamp was still burning. The men in the street took her and she did not come back until morning. She reached the doorstep of the old man's house and her hands found the threshold and she fell there. Her fingers were still wrapped around the wood when he opened the door.

The Levite opened the door in the morning to continue his journey. He saw her at the threshold and said: get up, let us go. She did not answer. He loaded her onto the donkey and went home. When he reached his house he took a knife. He cut her body into twelve pieces and sent one piece to each tribe of Israel. The smell of the road was still on his sandals when he did it.

All Israel received a piece. The message needed no explanation. Every tribe sent representatives. Four hundred thousand men gathered. They asked the Levite what happened. He told them about Gibeah. He told them about the men who came to the door. He said they tried to kill him. He said nothing else about her. The assembled tribes of Israel declared war on Benjamin over what happened that night.

The war was catastrophic. The tribe of Benjamin was nearly wiped from the earth over the events of one night in Gibeah. Thousands died on both sides over several battles. Cities burned. The smoke sat over the hills of Benjamin for days and the ash came down like grey snow. Israel had gone to war for a woman whose name the book never gave her.
